Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I'd study after lunch or in the evenings, but then I'd just end up scrolling through my phone or watching something. When I did study, it was usually because I had a deadline breathing down my neck. I know I spent like 29 hours a week supposedly studying, but a lot of that was just having materials open while my mind was elsewhere.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then when assignments were due I'd cram everything into a few stressful days. I never really established a consistent schedule because nothing felt urgent enough until it was almost too late. Deadlines were basically my only real motivator, which I know isn't great.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or just this vague guilt that I should be doing something productive. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was being a student, you know? I'd poke around, maybe watch part of a video, then get bored and close it. It wasn't really about wanting to learn most of the time.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were maybe a couple weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every morning with my coffee. But that faded fast. The platform just felt like another chore, and with my anxiety already being pretty high, forcing myself to engage felt exhausting. It never became automatic for me.
Q5: The lecture recordings were probably the most useful because I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other stuff, which made it feel less tedious. The discussion boards were okay too when people actually had interesting takes, but that was rare. Mostly I just needed someone to explain things out loud rather than reading walls of text.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and the supplementary PDFs. They just sat there in my downloads folder untouched. I also rarely did the optional quizzes because they felt pointless if they weren't graded. If something wasn't going to directly impact my grade, my brain just couldn't prioritize it over literally anything else.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly tied to an assignment or if I could see immediate results. Like, if watching a specific video would help me answer exam questions, I'd do it. But anything that felt like busywork or vague enrichment? I just couldn't bring myself to care. I needed clear, tangible payoff.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable to me. Required activities had this weight to them, even if I still procrastinated on them. Optional activities were like suggestions I could freely ignore, and I usually did. It's weird because I knew they might help, but without that external pressure, my motivation just evaporated.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone while walking or lying in bed. I also used some YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials. My notes from those sessions wouldn't show up anywhere in the LMS, but that's where some of my actual learning happened.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours listening to downloaded lectures or reviewing my handwritten notes, none of which the system would know about. I'd also talk through concepts with my roommate who was in a similar program. Those conversations helped me understand things, but the LMS just saw me as inactive during those periods.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a lecture once, then replay it multiple times offline over the next few days. The LMS would only register that initial access, not the fact that I listened to it three more times while trying to fall asleep. It's just easier to study without being connected sometimes.
Q12: Around midterms everything just felt overwhelming. My anxiety was through the roof, and I couldn't focus on anything. I'd sit down to study and my mind would just go blank or spiral into worrying about failing. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a little, but honestly, I was too stressed to even do those consistently.
Q13: Absolutely. I had extracurriculars that took up time, and when assignments piled up from multiple courses, this one always got pushed to the bottom. Fatigue was a big factor too—by evening I was too mentally drained to engage with complex material. The LMS became something I'd deal with 'later,' and later often never came.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd have videos playing but be completely zoned out, thinking about other stuff or just feeling too bored to actually absorb anything. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph five times without any of it sticking. Being logged in and being mentally present are two very different things for me.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important, feel anxious about it, and then avoid it because the anxiety made it feel impossible to start. It's this terrible cycle where the more important something is, the more I procrastinate because the pressure feels suffocating. I'd distract myself with easier, less important tasks instead.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd ignore everything until maybe two days before, then panic and try to throw something together. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and was basically dead otherwise. It's not a healthy pattern, but it's how I operated.
Q17: When I got feedback that was actually constructive, it motivated me a bit for the next assignment. But if feedback was vague or just a grade with no explanation, I'd feel deflated and less likely to try hard next time. Late feedback was basically useless because by then I'd already mentally moved on from that topic.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid and didn't really accommodate different learning styles. Everything was laid out week by week, but if you fell behind, catching up felt impossible. I needed more flexibility and maybe more audio-based content. The structure assumed everyone learns the same way, which just isn't true.
Q19: The heavy reliance on reading made it harder for me since I'm more of an auditory learner. Also, the sheer number of online courses I was juggling—seventeen total—made it impossible to give each one proper attention. If this course had more interactive or discussion-based elements, I might have engaged more naturally.
Q20: I think that's exactly me, actually. You can click around, watch videos, post in discussions, but if you're not mentally there, none of it translates to actual work getting done. High activity might just mean someone's going through the motions or procrastinating by doing easy tasks instead of the hard ones that matter.
Q21: Yeah, I can see that. Some people are just efficient—they grab what they need, study offline, and perform well without all the clicking around. The LMS doesn't capture studying from textbooks or discussing with classmates. Someone could look inactive but be learning in ways the system can't track.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss offline study, mental state, and whether engagement was genuine or just performative. A log is like a skeleton—it shows structure but not the actual life happening inside.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling like it matters. It's not just doing the minimum to pass but being curious and invested. For me, real engagement is rare—it happens when something clicks and I actually want to know more. Most of the time I'm just surviving, not engaging.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how anxious I felt every time I opened it, or how bored and disconnected I was even when I was technically active. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures on my phone or the conversations with friends that helped me understand things. My emotional state and offline efforts are completely invisible to the system.
